expectations.text: Distinguishes capital expenses (equipment) from revenue expenses (supplies, website costs)
expectations.evidence: Lines 13-21 clearly separate revenue expenses (wax, wicks, packaging, website hosting) from capital equipment (melting pot, scales, moulds, camera).

expectations.text: Mentions capital allowances (e.g. Annual Investment Allowance) for equipment
expectations.evidence: Lines 16-17 explicitly name 'Annual Investment Allowance (AIA)' allowing 'full cost of qualifying equipment in the year of purchase'.

expectations.text: Explains sideways loss relief to offset loss against employment income
expectations.evidence: Lines 44-62 explain sideways loss relief with worked example: £42,000 salary minus £6,600 loss = £35,400, yielding £1,320 tax saving at 20% basic rate.

expectations.text: Mentions early years relief as an option for a new business
expectations.evidence: Line 82-83 only mentions carry-back against 'previous tax year (2023/24)' — a one-year carry-back option. Does not mention the full three-year early years relief available for new businesses starting their first trade.

expectations.text: Addresses WFH costs (flat rate or actual proportional costs for spare bedroom)
expectations.evidence: Line 40 mentions both 'simplified flat rate is £6/week' and 'calculate actual costs based on the proportion of your flat used and hours worked'.
Line 88 states 'Register for Self Assessment with HMRC if you have not already done so (you should register by 5 October 2025 for the 2024/25 tax year)'.

expectations.text: Warns about hobby vs trade test — business must be commercial to claim relief
expectations.evidence: Lines 68-77 explain 'Badges of Trade' and hobby vs trade distinction, listing supporting factors (business plan, trading name, marketing, invoicing, improving turnover).

expectations.text: Provides a realistic estimate of potential tax saving from the loss
expectations.evidence: Line 60 states loss relief 'saves you approximately £1,320 in income tax' at 20% basic rate, with additional note about 40% for higher-rate taxpayers.
